content.problemStatement:
A suffix array is similar to prefix array. The only difference is that a suffix array works from right to left, while a prefix array works from left to right.

Suffix array of [1, 2, 3, 4, 5] -> [15, 14, 12, 9, 5]

We generally use prefix/suffix arrays to store the sum, but we can also use them to store some other operations like multiplication, GCD, OR, XOR, etc.

Some algorithms required us to use both prefix and suffix operations of different types together.

Problem
First line contains an integer NNN, the length of an array.
Second line contains NNN elements A1, A2, A3, . . . ANNN
Find the maximum GCD that can be obtained by removing one element from the array.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N, the length of the array. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1, A2, …, AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing the maximum possible GCD of the array after removing exactly one element.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Removing each element gives GCDs 1, 2, 1, 1 respectively; the maximum is 2.
All numbers are multiples of 2, so after removing any element the GCD remains at least 2 and cannot be larger.
All possible removals leave GCD 1, so the answer is 1.

solution.approach: For each position i we need the GCD of all elements except Ai. This can be obtained by precomputing prefix GCDs (gcd of A1..Ai) and suffix GCDs (gcd of Ai..AN). The GCD of the array without Ai is gcd(prefix[i‑1], suffix[i+1]). By scanning all i we keep the maximum of these values. The algorithm runs in O(N) per test case and uses O(N) extra memory for the two auxiliary arrays.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(total N) over all test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) auxiliary space for prefix and suffix arrays.
